features.name: Declarative Provisioning
features.description: Automates the deployment of GCP infrastructure using declarative configuration files, ensuring repeatability.

features.name: Managed Terraform Engine
features.description: Executes Terraform configurations in a fully managed cloud engine, maintaining state files securely.

features.name: Architectural Blueprints
features.description: Deploys pre-configured, Google-supported blueprints to construct validated enterprise environments.

iamRoles.description: Full access to administer and run managed infrastructure configurations.
Permissions to create projects required during infrastructure runs.

cliExamples.description: Initialize a managed infrastructure configuration run
cliExamples.command:
gcloud beta infra-manager deployments apply infrastructure-manager-infra \
  --location=us-central1 \
  --git-source-repo="https://github.com/my-org/gcp-infra" \
  --git-source-directory="terraform/dev"

cliExamples.description: Describe the details and state of a managed deployment
cliExamples.command: gcloud beta infra-manager deployments describe infrastructure-manager-infra --location=us-central1

design: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) ensures repeatable, documented, and audited cloud deployments.
Terraform is the industry-standard declarative tool for managing GCP resource lifecycles.
Infrastructure Manager is a managed engine that runs Terraform configurations, managing state files.
Deployment Manager is Google's native declarative template engine using YAML, Python, or Jinja2.
Use Terraform modules to bundle resource configurations (VPC, VMs) into reusable blocks.
Terraform state files must be stored in a remote backend (Cloud Storage) to enable collaboration.
Use state locking (native in Cloud Storage) to prevent parallel executions from corrupting state.
Application Design Center (ADC) drafts visual architecture designs, converting them to IaC scripts.
security: Do not hardcode service account keys or passwords in IaC scripts; pull them from Secret Manager.
Enforce access controls on the Cloud Storage bucket hosting the remote Terraform state file.
Enable object versioning on the state bucket to recover from accidental state corruptions.
Run IaC pipelines using a dedicated Service Account with the least privilege needed to create resources.
Audit every infrastructure deployment, change, and destroy operation in Cloud Audit Logs.
Use policy-as-code tools (like terraform-validator) to scan templates for security violations before runs.
VPC Service Controls protect state storage buckets from unauthorized cross-project access.
Implement branch protection on Git repositories hosting infrastructure code, requiring approvals.
reliability: Remote state files stored in Cloud Storage inherit 11 9s durability and multi-regional replication.
Terraform supports rolling back to a previous infrastructure version by reverting git commits.
Infrastructure Manager handles execution resources, preventing local connection drops from halting runs.
Always execute a 'terraform plan' to verify resource changes before running 'terraform apply'.
Enable logging on state buckets to audit which tools and users are accessing states.
Configure resource lifecycle rules in Terraform (e.g. prevent_destroy) to block accidental deletions of databases.
Store infrastructure code in a Git repository to ensure business continuity during disaster recovery.
Monitor deployment outcomes, errors, and apply statuses using Cloud Monitoring.
cost: Terraform is open-source and free; you pay only for the GCP resources provisioned.
Infrastructure Manager charges based on deployment runs and active state management operations.
Use dry-run options ('terraform plan') to preview resources, avoiding unwanted billing from typos.
Consolidate multiple state files where appropriate to reduce Cloud Storage bucket fees.
Automate the destruction of temporary test environments using cron triggers and pipelines.
Review provisioned resources in state files to identify oversized VM shapes and idle disks.
Use Terraform local-exec commands sparingly to avoid spun-up VM resource overhead.
Analyze cost differences between regional and multi-regional state storage buckets.
